objectives: Describe and distinguish the programming constructs (sequence, selection, count- and condition-controlled iteration), recursion, scope, modularity and OOP
Explain how recursion uses a base case and a call stack of frames, and contrast it with iteration
Apply scope rules, parameter passing (by value vs by reference) and OOP concepts (encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism) when reading and writing OCR pseudocode

retrieval.q: Which loop type tests its condition at the end and so always runs at least once?
retrieval.a: A post-conditioned loop (do...until); a while loop is pre-conditioned and may run zero times.

retrieval.q: What is the single defining difference between a function and a procedure?
retrieval.a: A function returns a single value (usable in an expression); a procedure returns no value.

retrieval.q: When an argument is passed by reference, what is actually passed and what is the consequence?
retrieval.a: The memory address of the original variable is passed, so changes inside the subroutine change the original.

retrieval.q: State the difference between the MAR and the MDR (interleaves 1.1.1 processor registers).
retrieval.a: The MAR holds the address of a memory location; the MDR holds the data or instruction at that location.

retrieval.q: What does a stack do on a push, and in what order are items removed (interleaves 1.4.2 data structures)?
retrieval.a: A push adds an item to the top; items are removed top-first in LIFO (last in, first out) order.

keywords.def: A subroutine that calls itself, needing a base case to stop and a general/recursive case that moves closer to it.
The condition that stops recursion with no further recursive call; if unreached it causes a stack overflow.
A stack of frames holding the parameters, local variables and return address for each active subroutine call.
The region of a program in which a variable can be accessed (is visible).
A variable declared inside a subroutine, visible only within it and existing only while it runs.
A variable declared outside any subroutine, visible and usable anywhere in the program for its whole run.
By value passes a copy so the original is unchanged; by reference passes the address so the original can be changed.
Bundling data and methods in a class with private attributes accessed only via public methods (data hiding).

content.heading: Constructs and iteration
points: Sequence (order matters), selection (if/elseif/else, switch/case) and iteration are the building blocks of any program
Count-controlled iteration (for...next) repeats a fixed, known number of times; condition-controlled (while, do...until) repeats an unknown number of times
while is pre-conditioned (tests at the start, may run zero times); do...until is post-conditioned (tests at the end, runs at least once, good for input validation)

content.heading: Recursion and the call stack
points: A recursive subroutine calls itself and must have a base case (stops recursion) plus a general case (calls itself, moving toward the base case)
Each call pushes a stack frame storing parameters, local variables and the return address; frames are popped in reverse (LIFO) as calls return
A missing or unreached base case causes endless calls and a stack overflow; recursion uses more memory than iteration but suits self-similar problems like tree traversal and factorial

content.heading: Scope: local vs global
points: A local variable is declared inside a subroutine and is visible only there; a global is declared in the main program and is visible everywhere
Prefer local variables: fewer side effects, memory freed on exit, and code that is more modular, reusable and easier to debug
A local variable shadows a same-named global within its subroutine, so an assignment to it does not change the global

content.heading: Modularity, functions and parameters
points: Modularity splits a program into self-contained subroutines (modules), making it easier to write, test, debug, reuse and share between a team
A procedure performs a task and returns no value; a function performs a task and returns a single value usable in an expression
A parameter is the variable in the definition; an argument is the value supplied at the call; pass by reference is efficient for large data and can return changes, by value is safer

content.heading: Object-oriented programming
points: A class is a template defining attributes and methods; an object is a specific instance of a class created at run time
Encapsulation keeps attributes private and exposes public getters/setters (data hiding protects integrity); a constructor (OCR: new) initialises attributes
Inheritance lets a subclass acquire a superclass's attributes and methods (models is-a); polymorphism makes the same method call behave differently per object type, usually via overriding

worked_example.title: Tracing factorial(4) on the call stack
steps: function factorial(n): if n <= 1 then return 1 else return n * factorial(n - 1)
Call factorial(4): not base case, so push a frame and call factorial(3)
Call factorial(3): push a frame and call factorial(2); call factorial(2): push a frame and call factorial(1)
factorial(1): n <= 1 is true, so the base case returns 1 with no further call
Frames now pop in LIFO order: factorial(2) returns 2*1 = 2, factorial(3) returns 3*2 = 6, factorial(4) returns 4*6 = 24
The stack is now empty and the final result returned to the caller is 24

task: Trace the call factorial(4) using the routine above: draw the call stack showing each frame as it is pushed, mark where the base case is reached, then show each value returned as the frames pop, ending with the final answer 24.
challenge: Given procedure addOne(byRef n) and procedure addOne(byVal n) each doing n = n + 1, and a variable x = 3, state the value of x after each call and explain the difference in terms of consequence (not just that a copy is made). Then rewrite factorial as an iterative for...next loop and state one advantage of each version.

exit_ticket.q: Why does a recursive routine with no reachable base case crash, and what is the error called?
exit_ticket.a: Each call pushes another stack frame and they never stop, so memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow.

exit_ticket.q: A global total = 10; inside a procedure an assignment total = 5 then print(total) runs, then print(total) runs in the main program. What two values print and why?
exit_ticket.a: 5 then 10: the assignment creates a local total (scope = the procedure) that shadows but does not change the global, which stays 10.

exit_ticket.q: In OCR pseudocode, why must getBalance() be written as a function rather than a procedure?
exit_ticket.a: It returns a single value (the balance), and only a function returns a value; a procedure would return nothing.
